Based on the search results, here's what I found about vLLM's CUDA graph configuration parameters:

## cudagraph_mode

The cudagraph_mode controls whether vLLM uses full CUDA graphs for uniform decode, piecewise graphs for other batch types, with FULL_AND_PIECEWISE being the default mode for better performance (though for pooling models it defaults to PIECEWISE).

FULL_DECODE_ONLY is a mode suitable for decode instances where prefill is not as important, as it uses full CUDA graphs for uniform decode only and no cudagraph for prefill/mixed batches, saving memory needed for PIECEWISE CUDA graphs.

## max_cudagraph_capture_size

A cudagraph captured for a specific size can only be used for the same size, so vLLM needs to capture all the sizes it wants to use. The max_cudagraph_capture_size parameter limits which sizes need to be captured for CUDA graph compilation.

## Pooling and Embedding Support

For pooling models, vLLM defaults to PIECEWISE mode rather than the FULL_AND_PIECEWISE mode used for standard language models, indicating special handling for pooling embeddings models.

The search results show that these parameters are part of vLLM's CompilationConfig for managing CUDA graph performance and memory usage.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding these vLLM concepts:

## enforce_eager

The `--enforce-eager` flag turns off the vLLM-torch.compile integration and runs entirely in eager mode, including turning off CUDAGraphs.

## compile_config / compilation_config

You can use `--compilation_config` with vLLM to specify compile sizes, for example: `--compilation_config '{"compile_sizes": [1, 2, 4, 8]}'` to compile specific kernels for batch sizes 1, 2, 4, and 8.

## Inductor and max-autotune

vLLM has environment variables `VLLM_ENABLE_INDUCTOR_MAX_AUTOTUNE` and `VLLM_ENABLE_INDUCTOR_COORDINATE_DESCENT_TUNING` to enable max_autotune and coordinate_descent_tuning in the inductor_config for compiling static shapes passed from compile_sizes, both enabled by default.

When all shapes in the computation graph are static and known, auto-tuning is turned on to tune for maximum performance, though this can be slow on the first run but subsequent runs can bypass tuning and run the tuned kernel directly.

## Pooling

vLLM supports overriding or setting the pooling method for pooling models with configuration like `{"pooling_type": "mean", "normalize": false}`.

## Attention Backend Configuration

You can specify the attention backend in vLLM using either AttentionConfig with an enum or the attention_backend parameter with a string, such as for Qwen3-0.6B.

## VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND Environment Variable

The VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND environment variable is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0 or v1.0.0, with the recommendation to use the --attention-config.backend command line argument or AttentionConfig(backend=...) config field instead.

## FlashInfer and Qwen3-VL Issues

There are several known issues with FlashInfer and Qwen3-VL models:

## Performance Regression Issues

Embedding quality declined significantly between vLLM version 0.14.0rc2.dev199+gc80f92c14 (where embeddings were high quality) and version 0.15.2.dev0+g1892993bc (where embeddings became low quality).

Based on the search results, here's what I found about vLLM multimodal encoder cache, mm_cache flags, and Qwen3-VL:

## Encoder Cache Management

The EncoderCacheManager manages caching of encoder outputs for multimodal models in vLLM V1, handling the lifecycle of multimodal encoder outputs (such as vision embeddings from images) during request processing. It provides memory-aware caching to avoid recomputing encoder outputs when the same multimodal inputs appear in different stages of request processing.

## Qwen3-VL Specific Considerations

The Qwen3-VL release introduces a challenge to the encoder cache design, particularly for video inference because of the new timestamp insertion where the special tokens impact cache memory consumption. You can enable text-only mode by passing --limit-mm-per-prompt.video 0 --limit-mm-per-prompt.image 0, which skips the vision encoder and multimodal profiling to free up memory for additional KV cache.

## Key Flags for Multimodal Optimization

For multimodal workloads with Qwen3-VL, use --mm-encoder-tp-mode data for data-parallel vision encoding and --mm-processor-cache-type shm to efficiently cache and transfer preprocessed multimodal inputs in shared memory.

## Embedding Cache Features

ECConnector is vLLM's native connector for transferring multimodal embeddings via an Embedding Cache, where the encoder worker acts as a producer (writes embeddings), while the PD worker acts as a consumer (reads embeddings).

I've completed the web search. Here are the key findings about vLLM, Snowflake Arctic Inference, embedding production optimization:

## Overview

Arctic Inference is an open-source plugin for vLLM that implements a suite of inference optimizations originally developed at Snowflake. The system extends vLLM's functionality without requiring a fork, using Python's entry point mechanism to inject itself into vLLM's execution pipeline, and once installed, Arctic Inference automatically patches vLLM components to enable optimizations while maintaining full compatibility with existing vLLM APIs and CLI commands.

## Performance Improvements for Embeddings

Arctic Inference with vLLM delivers 1.6M tokens/sec per GPU for embeddings and performs 2.4× faster than Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) on short sequences and at parity for long sequences. By optimizing serialization, tokenization and GPU utilization, Snowflake increased throughput by 16x on short sequences and 4.2x on long sequences, while reducing cost per token 16x on short sequences.

## Key Optimizations

Three key optimizations were implemented: encoding embedding vectors as little-endian bytes for faster serialization, disaggregating tokenization and inference into a pipeline, and running multiple model replicas on single GPUs.

## Production Impact

Arctic Inference powers key workloads in Snowflake Cortex AI including Snowflake Llama models and embedding models in Snowflake Cortex AI. The throughput gains are rolling out across Cortex, powering faster indexing and lower-latency responses across products like Cortex Agents, Cortex Search and Cortex Analyst.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about vLLM's prefix caching, chunked prefill, and related features:

## Key Findings

When chunked prefill is enabled, a long prompt is split into multiple chunks, and only the first chunk leverages prefix caching during execution, which may result in suboptimal cache utilization.

vLLM supports an experimental feature called chunked prefill, which allows chunking large prefills into smaller chunks and batching them together with decode requests.

## Integration Challenges

There have been specific issues with combining these features. In PR #12639, new variables related to chunked prefill and a function called _compute_prefill_context were introduced in the MLACommonBackend class. The _compute_prefill_context function is called when prefix caching is enabled and uses variables related to chunked prefill, but if chunked prefill is not enabled, those variables will be None and cause assertion errors.

The search results also reference PR #12642 which addresses "Disable chunked prefill and/or prefix caching when MLA is enabled," indicating there are compatibility considerations between these features and certain model architectures.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about vLLM's `cudagraph_mode`, `FULL_DECODE_ONLY`, and pooling models:

## FULL_DECODE_ONLY Mode

FULL_DECODE_ONLY captures full CUDA Graph for uniform decode, with no cudagraph for prefill/mixed operations. It's suitable for decode instances in a P/D (prefill/decode) setup where prefill is not as important, allowing you to save the memory needed for PIECEWISE CUDA Graphs.

## Default for Pooling Models

Regarding the default `cudagraph_mode` for pooling models specifically, for pooling models, the default is still PIECEWISE (rather than the standard v1 default of FULL_AND_PIECEWISE for other models).

## General Default Behavior

FULL_AND_PIECEWISE is the default mode for general use, which captures full CUDA Graphs for uniform decode and piecewise CUDA Graphs for prefill/mixed batches, but pooling models still default to PIECEWISE.

Based on the search results, here's information about vLLM throughput tuning with `max_num_seqs` and `max_num_batched_tokens`:

## Key Parameters

**max_num_seqs**: This parameter governs the maximum number of sequences (individual requests or their ongoing generations) that vLLM can process concurrently within a single batch and is crucial for managing GPU memory and throughput in vLLM's continuous batching system.

**max_num_batched_tokens**: This sets an upper limit on the total number of tokens that can be processed in one iteration (one scheduler step).

## Throughput Optimization

For throughput, it's recommended to set max_num_batched_tokens > 2048. More specifically, sweep --max-num-batched-tokens around 8k–64k to find your latency/throughput balance.

If the system has enough available GPU memory, setting max-num-batched-tokens as high as possible yields better throughput and lower latency, improving overall serving performance.

## Memory Management Trade-offs

Decreasing max_num_seqs or max_num_batched_tokens reduces the number of concurrent requests in a batch, thereby requiring less KV cache space. This is useful when you encounter memory constraints.

## Embedding & Chunked Prefill

Chunked prefill allows vLLM to process large prefills in smaller chunks and batch them together with decode requests, helping improve both throughput and latency by better balancing compute-bound (prefill) and memory-bound (decode) operations.

## VLLM_USE_V1 and Encoder Cache

vLLM V1 enables chunked-prefill scheduling for multimodal models with the "encoder cache." In V0, image inputs and text inputs had to be processed in the same step, but with the encoder cache, V1 temporarily stores vision embeddings, allowing the scheduler to split text inputs into chunks and process them across multiple steps without needing to regenerate vision embeddings every step.

## Encoder Cache Functionality

The EncoderCacheManager manages caching of encoder outputs for multimodal models in vLLM V1. It handles the lifecycle of multimodal encoder outputs (such as vision embeddings from images) during request processing and provides memory-aware caching to avoid recomputing encoder outputs when the same multimodal inputs appear in different stages of request processing.

## Pooling Support

vLLM currently supports four types of pooling tasks: embed, classify, score, and reward. The encode task has been split into two more specific token-wise tasks: token_embed (using normalization as activation) and token_classify (using softmax as the activation by default).

# Qwen3-VL Performance Optimization Flags

Based on the vLLM documentation, here are the key optimization knobs for Qwen3-VL inference:

## Core Parallelization Flags
- `--tensor-parallel-size` (8 for 235B model on most configs)
- `--enable-expert-parallel` (enables MoE expert deployment across GPUs)
- `--mm-encoder-tp-mode data` (deploys vision encoder in data-parallel fashion for better performance)

## Memory & Cache Management
- `--max-model-len` (reduce from default 262K; 128K recommended for most scenarios)
- `--gpu-memory-utilization` (0.95 for image-only inference; adjust if using `--mm-encoder-tp-mode data`)
- `--mm-processor-cache-gb 0` (for unique multimodal inputs)
- `--mm-processor-cache-type shm` (experimental shared memory caching for better performance at high TP)
- `--skip-mm-profiling` (skip memory profiling, lower `--gpu-memory-utilization` accordingly)

## Input Constraints
- `--limit-mm-per-prompt.video 0` (disable video processing if image-only)
- `--limit-mm-per-prompt.image 0` (text-only mode with skipped vision encoder)

## Scheduling & Performance
- `--async-scheduling` (overlaps scheduling with decoding; note compatibility limitations with speculative decoding in vLLM <0.11.1)
- `--max-num-seqs 128` (batch size configuration)

## Environment Variables
- `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` (limits preprocessing threads, reduces CPU contention)
- `MIOPEN_FIND_MODE=FAST`, `VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER=1`, `SAFETENSORS_FAST_GPU=1` (AMD-specific optimizations)

## Precision Options
- FP8 checkpoint for H100 (optimal memory efficiency)
- BF16 for standard inference

# Technical Mechanisms for Scaling vLLM Embedding Throughput

Based on the Snowflake engineering blog post, here are the specific optimization techniques used:

## 1. **Multiple Model Replicas Per GPU**
Running "multiple replicas of the same model on a single GPU" increases throughput by better utilizing idle GPU resources. The approach leverages underutilized streaming multiprocessors (SMs) that remain idle when embedding models run sequentially, enabling concurrent request serving.

## 2. **Disaggregated Tokenization and Inference**
Rather than sequential processing, the system "pretokenize inputs and pass token IDs directly to vLLM." This enables "pipeline parallelism; tokenization and inference can run in parallel across different requests." Tokenization for subsequent requests overlaps with GPU inference on prior requests, eliminating GPU idle time during CPU-based tokenization.

## 3. **Serialization Optimization**
Embeddings are encoded "as raw bytes in little-endian" format using NumPy vectorization to avoid the GIL overhead and lack of SIMD support in Python Protobuf. The approach converts output vectors to bytes using `astype(dtype="<f4").tobytes()` for faster gRPC serialization.

## Notable Results
These three mechanisms combined delivered 3x throughput improvement in production Snowflake Cortex AI, reaching 230,000 tokens per second on A10g GPUs, and up to 16x improvement on H200 hardware for short sequences.

The optimizations are available in the open-source Arctic Inference project.

## Overview

vLLM v0.11.0 changed the default CUDAGraphMode from PIECEWISE to FULL_AND_PIECEWISE. This should provide better out of the box performance for most models, particularly fine-grained MoEs, while preserving compatibility with existing models supporting only PIECEWISE mode.

## Known Issues

However, there are several compatibility issues that have been reported:

1. **FlexAttention Backend Incompatibility**: There's an error where CUDAGraphMode.FULL_AND_PIECEWISE is not supported with FlexAttentionMetadataBuilder backend, requiring users to fall back to PIECEWISE mode.

2. **Llama 4 Family Issues**: Llama 4 model family suffers from gibberish outputs for long context, which can be recovered by setting --compilation-config {"cudagraph_mode":"PIECEWISE"}.

3. **NaN Logits Problem**: Issues are related to FULL_AND_PIECEWISE cudagraph mode, occurring with vllm v0.11.2, where NaNs in logits cause invalid output.

## Special Handling for Pooling Models

For pooling models, it's still PIECEWISE rather than FULL_AND_PIECEWISE, which addresses the specific pooling compatibility issue mentioned in your query.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about vLLM's async scheduling, pooling, embedding, overlap, and output processing:

## Async Scheduling and Overlap

vLLM v0.14.0 enables async scheduling by default to overlap CPU-side scheduling work with GPU execution. This zero-bubble overlap approach means the scheduler prepares the next batch while the current batch is still executing on the GPU, eliminating idle time between steps.

The model runner can function asynchronously when using async scheduling, which allows full overlap of the CPU operations (including prepare_inputs) and the model forward pass.

## Output Processing

Async output processing can be disabled, though this may result in lower performance. Overlapped model execution allows the model runner to return a CUDA tensor reference to the sampled token ids, instead of the pythonized token ids.

## Pooling and Embeddings

vLLM supports pooling models with various tasks. The embed method outputs an embedding vector for each prompt and is primarily designed for embedding models. Pooling parameters can be specified for requests.

## Performance Impact

Expected speedup from the async scheduling and overlap features is 5-10% over the current async scheduling.

# ViT CUDA Graphs for Qwen3-VL: Configuration & Performance

## Enabling Flags & Configuration

**Primary flag:** `cudagraph_mm_encoder` (boolean, default `False`) in `CompilationConfig`

**Optional parameters:**
- `encoder_cudagraph_token_budgets` (list of integers) — override auto-inferred budget levels
- `encoder_cudagraph_max_vision_items_per_batch` (integer) — maximum images/videos per batch
- `encoder_cudagraph_max_frames_per_batch` (integer) — for video support

**CLI example:**
```bash
vllm serve Qwen/Qwen3-VL-32B \
  --compilation-config '{"cudagraph_mm_encoder": true}'
```

## Expected Speedup

On single Blackwell GPU (GB200) with Qwen3-VL-30B:
- **FLASH_ATTN:** +11.8% mean latency (5.13→4.52ms), +31.6% P99 (9.16→6.26ms)
- **FLASHINFER:** +19.6% mean latency (5.42→4.36ms), +40.3% P99 (10.87→6.49ms)

Multi-GPU (4x TP=4, DP=4) shows larger gains: up to +44.4% mean and +84.9% P99 with FLASHINFER.

## Correctness & Contract Risks

**Key design constraint:** "Encoder CUDA Graphs are orthogonal to decoder CUDA Graphs — both can be enabled simultaneously." This isolation reduces interaction complexity.

**Validation mechanism:** Greedy bin-packing at runtime handles variable input sizes. Images exceeding all budgets "fall back to eager execution," ensuring correctness over performance.

**Video limitation:** "Video CUDA graphs are automatically disabled when EVS (Efficient Video Sampling) pruning is enabled, since EVS makes the token count data-dependent."

## Graph Capture Timing

Graphs are "pre-captured at multiple token budget levels during model initialization," before serving requests. The system captures at power-of-2 budget levels auto-inferred from model architecture unless user-specified values override defaults.

## VLLM_DISABLE_COMPILE_CACHE

You can disable the compilation cache by setting the environment variable VLLM_DISABLE_COMPILE_CACHE=1 if you want to debug the compilation process or suspect the cache is causing issues. The cache is enabled by default.

## Compilation and Warmup

A unique aspect of vLLM's torch.compile integration is that all compilation is guaranteed to finish before serving any requests, and no requests will trigger new compilations. There's a feature flag `VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE` to enable/disable AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compilation, which ensures compilation is done in the warmup phase and reused in subsequent calls.

## Cache Reliability

While torch.compile's compiler cache is rock-stable, vLLM's compiler cache is unfortunately not always correct. vLLM computes the cache key by combining multiple factors like config flags and model name, and if the cache is wrong, it usually means that a factor is missing.

## Key Findings:

**torch.compile in vLLM V1:**
In vLLM's V1 architecture, torch.compile is enabled by default and is a critical part of the framework.

**Compilation and Caching:**
vLLM compiles models during cold start and saves the artifacts (FX graphs, Triton kernels) in a cache directory (by default, ~/.cache/vllm/torch_compile_cache). On warm start, the artifacts are retrieved from the cache.

**Warmup and Performance:**
You can directly copy the whole ~/.cache/vllm/torch_compile_cache directory in your deployment scenario to save a great amount of compilation time, and hence accelerating the starting time of the vLLM instance.

**Auto-tuning:**
Because auto-tuning takes quite a long time (from seconds to minutes, depending on the model size and the batch size), even though it can be cached for later use, for the sake of user-friendliness, we turn it off by default.

## max_num_seqs Parameter

The maximum number of sequences parameter controls the batch size by sequence count, affecting throughput and memory usage. More specifically, `max_num_seqs` governs the maximum number of sequences that vLLM can process concurrently within a single batch and is a crucial parameter for managing GPU memory and throughput in vLLM's continuous batching system.

For example, if max_num_seqs=8, up to 8 different prompts can be handled at once, regardless of their individual lengths, as long as the total token count also fits within the Max Number of Batched Tokens.

## Important Note on Concurrency

max_num_seqs is an upper limit, not actual concurrency. vLLM determines the true concurrency dynamically based on available KV cache.

# vLLM PR #41163: AllPool.forward Optimization

**Optimized Method:** `AllPool.forward` in the token-wise pooling layer

**Optimization Mechanism:** The PR replaces individual slicing operations on hidden states with a single group slice followed by a split operation based on per-request token counts. This reduces overhead by batching slice operations.

**Performance Improvement:** "51% faster in the method level benchmark" - median latency decreased from 59.89 microseconds to 39.65 microseconds

**Benchmark Details:** 
- Configuration: 64 sequences (128 tokens each), hidden size 4096, bfloat16 dtype
- 32 sequences use token classification task requiring per-token processing
- 4000 iterations measured across 5 runs on CUDA

**Versions:** Merged April 29, 2026 into the main branch (vLLM v0.6.x+)

**User-Facing Changes:** None - this is an internal optimization with no API or configuration changes required

# GPU-CPU Sync Elimination in vLLM Pooling

## Mechanism

The PR eliminates unnecessary GPU-to-CPU synchronizations by performing index calculations and metadata operations on the CPU instead. Specifically:

- **Sequence-wise pooling**: Segment IDs are generated on the CPU
- **Token-wise pooling**: CPU-based token IDs are used for masking operations

This approach keeps intermediate calculations on the CPU, avoiding expensive synchronization points between GPU and CPU.

## Call Sites

The changes target the pooling code in vLLM, specifically modifying:
- `vllm/model_executor/layers/pooler/tokwise/methods.py`
- Related pooling implementations for sequence-wise operations

## Performance Data

Unfortunately, the provided content does not include specific speedup metrics or benchmarking results. The PR description mentions this is "the second batch" of sync optimizations (part 2 of n), suggesting iterative improvements, but concrete performance numbers are not documented in the available GitHub discussion.

## `--limit-mm-per-prompt`

This parameter allows you to limit the maximum number of multimodal items per prompt to reduce the memory footprint of the model, such as accepting up to 3 images and 1 video per prompt.

It's highly recommended to specify `--limit-mm-per-prompt.video 0` if your inference server will only process image inputs since enabling video inputs consumes more memory reserved for long video embeddings.

You can enable text-only mode by passing `--limit-mm-per-prompt.video 0 --limit-mm-per-prompt.image 0`, which skips the vision encoder and multimodal profiling to free up memory for additional KV cache.

## `--skip-mm-profiling`

When enabled, this option skips multimodal memory profiling and only profiles with the language backbone model during engine initialization. This reduces engine startup time but shifts the responsibility to users for estimating the peak memory usage of the activation of multimodal encoder and embedding cache.

## Video Embedding & Pooling

Video embedding pooling can be used with vLLM's `runner="pooling"` mode along with `limit_mm_per_prompt` settings, as shown in the embedding examples for multimodal models.

These features work together to optimize memory usage when running multimodal models with video and image inputs.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about vLLM's `cudagraph_capture_sizes` parameter:

## Overview

You can specify the full compilation config like so: `{"mode": 3, "cudagraph_capture_sizes": [1, 2, 4, 8]}`.

## How it Works

A cudagraph captured for a specific size can only be used for the same size, and you need to capture all the sizes you want to use.

## Configuration Options

For sizes to capture cudagraph: None (default) means capture sizes are inferred from vllm config, or you can specify capture sizes as a list of integers.

If cudagraph_capture_sizes is not specified, the list of sizes is generated automatically following the pattern: [1, 2, 4] + list(range(8, 256, 8)) + list(range(256, max_cudagraph_capture_size + 1, 16)).

## Use Cases

Users can customize cudagraph capture sizes to accelerate start time, while only capturing cudagraph for certain sizes they care about.

## Special Note for Pooling Models

For pooling models, the default mode is still PIECEWISE (rather than FULL_AND_PIECEWISE).

The search results don't contain specific information about using a batch size of 100 with pooling embeddings, but the general principle is that you can override the default capture sizes by specifying your own list in the compilation config.
